pattern: Bitwise AND only ever turns bits off as a window grows, so the set of distinct AND values ending at any position stays tiny (bounded by the bit width) and can be carried forward instead of recomputing every subarray from scratch.
hints: For a fixed right endpoint, the running ANDs of every subarray ending there can only decrease (in the bitwise sense) as the left endpoint moves further left, so there are at most ~30 distinct values, not O(n) of them.
Keep a small set of 'all AND values of subarrays ending at the previous index'; extending to the new index just ANDs each of those with the new number and adds the number itself.
Track the minimum |value - target| seen over every distinct AND value produced at every position - the answer is the smallest of all of those.

edge_inputs.description: several distinct values with a mid-range target
all identical large values far from a small target
powers of two whose AND collapses to zero, matching a zero target exactly
max value repeated against the max target
the target is exactly achievable by a single element
all-ones bit pattern already equal to the target

stress.description: small arrays that exercise the independent quadratic brute-force oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1500))
    arr = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    target = rng.randint(0, 10000000)
    return [arr, target]

stress.description: large random arrays near the maximum length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    arr = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    target = rng.randint(0, 10000000)
    return [arr, target]
